children.value: What the Grid of Numbers Actually Means

children.label: What the Grid of Numbers Actually Means

children.value: Chapter 2 explained how the sensor produces one integer per photosite. This chapter asks: what is that integer, what are its limits, and what happens when you try to represent too much with too few?
3.1 What a Pixel Is — and Is Not

children.label: 3.1 What a Pixel Is — and Is Not

children.value: : the average brightness measured by one photosite at one grid location. It has:

children.html: (i,j)(i, j)(i,j)
iii
jjj

children.value: — it is just a number, not a tiny photograph
When a screen renders an image it draws each pixel as a coloured square. That is a display convention, not a property of the data. The underlying data is a 2D array of integers.

zooming into a digital image reveals more detail. It does not — it only makes the squares bigger. All the detail that exists was fixed at capture time by the sensor’s spatial sampling rate (Chapter 1).
= the number of pixels: width × height. More pixels means finer spatial sampling, which preserves more scene detail.
But pixels alone say nothing about real-world scale. The same 1000×1000 pixel image could represent a postage stamp or a football field. What matters for computer vision is the

children.html: GSD=physical scene width (mm)image width (pixels)\text{GSD} = \frac{\text{physical scene width (mm)}}{\text{image width (pixels)}}GSD=image width (pixels)physical scene width (mm)​

children.value: GSD is the real-world size each pixel represents. It determines the smallest detectable feature:

children.html: minimum detectable feature≥2×GSD\text{minimum detectable feature} \geq 2 \times \text{GSD}minimum detectable feature≥2×GSD

children.value: (The factor of 2 is the Nyquist criterion from Chapter 1, applied to spatial resolution.)
After the sensor measures a voltage proportional to electron count, the
maps it to a discrete integer. This mapping — rounding a continuous value to the nearest level — is

children.value: levels. The step size between adjacent levels is:

children.html: Δ=Vmax⁡2B−1\Delta = \frac{V_{\max}}{2^B - 1}Δ=2B−1Vmax​​

children.value: The quantization error is at most

children.html: ±Δ/2\pm \Delta/2±Δ/2

children.value: — unlike shot noise (random), it cannot be averaged away. Once the
rounds a value, the fractional part is gone.
When bit depth is too low, smooth gradients in the scene produce
— visible step edges where the true scene varies smoothly.

A 2-bit image has only 4 grey levels (0, 85, 170, 255). A smooth gradient from 0 to 255 jumps in steps of 85 intensity units. Each step is visible as a hard edge — a feature that doesn’t exist in the scene.
False contours are the spatial equivalent of quantization noise. They disappear when bit depth is increased to 8-bit or beyond.

children.value: Both corrupt pixel values independently of scene content. We have not yet accounted for contrast changes, shading, colour processing, or compression — those come in Chapters 4–5 and dominate the problem in Chapter 6.
